frameworks.name: bounded evidence accumulation
frameworks.one_line: Noisy momentary evidence integrated to a threshold determines choice, RT and confidence.

frameworks.name: drift-diffusion
frameworks.one_line: Diffusion of a decision variable used to model the volatility effect quantitatively.

frameworks.name: signal-detection theory
frameworks.one_line: Background framing for signal-to-noise effects on perceptual accuracy.

core_question: Does selectively increasing the variance of momentary sensory evidence speed decisions and inflate confidence as bounded accumulation predicts?
key_assumptions: Confidence derives from a mapping between decision variable state and probability correct
MT/MST neuronal responses represent the momentary motion evidence accumulated by the decision process
The volatility manipulation leaves mean evidence unchanged while raising its variance
manipulations: Doubly stochastic random-dot motion: frame-by-frame variation of coherence (high vs low volatility)
Species comparison: humans rating confidence vs monkeys using opt-out / sure-bet
key_findings: Neuronal recordings in MT/MST confirmed the manipulation raised response variance with little mean change
Higher volatility produced faster reaction times in both humans and monkeys
Higher volatility raised confidence over a range where accuracy was minimally impaired
interpretations: Results quantitatively match a bounded evidence accumulation model with increased diffusion noise
The brain does not always read out evidence reliability to recalibrate confidence and speed
limitations_noted: Volatility levels were randomly interleaved, so observers may not learn to adjust the DV-to-confidence mapping per condition
